title: LLM Fundamentals
description: Test your knowledge of how large language models work

questions.question: What is a 'token' in a large language model?
options: A chunk of text — often a word or sub-word — that the model reads and generates
A security key used to log in
A single pixel in an image
A unit of GPU memory
questions.explanation: LLMs process text as tokens (words or sub-word pieces); both input and output are measured in tokens.

questions.question: At its core, how does an LLM generate text?
options: By predicting the next token repeatedly, one at a time
By copying the closest matching sentence from a database
By running a fixed set of if/else rules
By translating everything to English first
questions.explanation: An LLM generates autoregressively: it predicts the most likely next token given everything so far, then repeats with that token appended.

questions.question: What is a 'hallucination' in the context of LLMs?
options: The model producing confident output that is factually wrong or made up
The model refusing to answer
The model running out of memory
The model responding in the wrong language
questions.explanation: A hallucination is fluent, confident-sounding output that is not grounded in fact — a known failure mode of generative models.

questions.question: What distinguishes 'fine-tuning' from 'prompting'?
options: Fine-tuning updates the model's weights on new data; prompting only changes the input
They are two names for the same thing
Prompting permanently retrains the model
Fine-tuning is done by the end user at inference time
questions.explanation: Fine-tuning further trains the model on task-specific data, changing its parameters, whereas prompting leaves the model unchanged and only adjusts what you send it.

questions.question: What is the 'transformer' in modern LLMs?
options: The neural network architecture, based on attention, that underpins most LLMs
A device that converts electrical voltage
A tool that transforms JSON into CSV
The part of the API that formats the response
questions.explanation: The transformer is the attention-based neural architecture introduced in 2017 that powers today's large language models.
